Microelectronics Journal  
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/mejo  
Delay-correlation-aware SSTA based on conditional moments  
Zeqin Wu a, Philippe Maurine a, Nadine Azemard a,n, Gilles R. Ducharme b  
a LIRMM, UMR CNRS/University of Montpellier II, 161 rue Ada, 34095 Montpellier Cedex 5, France  
b
ꢀ
Dept. Math University of Montpellier II, Place Eugene Bataillon, 34095 Montpellier, France  
a r t i c l e i n f o  
a b s t r a c t  
Article history:  
Corner-based Timing Analysis (CTA) becomes more and more pessimistic as feature size shrinks. This  
trend has motivated the development of Statistical Static Timing Analysis (SSTA). In this paper, we  
propose a new path-based SSTA framework that allows the estimation of path delay distributions and  
delay correlations by propagating iteratively mean and variance of cell delay. These moments,  
conditioned on input slope and output load values, are pre-characterized by an improved method:  
log-logistic distribution based input signals and inverters as output load. In applications, the delay  
gains of this SSTA framework with respect to CTA are shown to be signiﬁcant. It is also highlighted that  
the discrepancy of critical paths orderings obtained by SSTA and CTA depends on two factors: cell-to-  
cell delay correlation and standard deviation of cell delay.  
Received 28 June 2011  
Received in revised form  
5 January 2012  
Accepted 6 January 2012  
Available online 25 January 2012  
Keywords:  
Statistical static timing analysis  
Moments propagation  
Cell-to-cell delay correlation  
Path-to-path delay correlation  
& 2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.  
1. Introduction  
A second weakness is the lack of an effective technique to  
estimate correlations between cell delays and between path  
As process technology enters the nanometer era (o90 nm),  
Corner-based Timing Analysis (CTA) reaches its limits in construct-  
ing guaranteed bounds on circuit delays and becomes overly  
conservative. This pessimism comes from the assumption that all  
physical and environmental parameters are at their worst or best  
conditions simultaneously. In other words, extreme values are  
used to replace random process parameters and all correlations  
between them are set to either ‘‘1’’ or ‘‘À1’’. More importantly,  
this pessimism increases as feature size shrinks. Statistical Static  
Timing Analysis (SSTA) offers a way out of this problem and has  
been much developed in recent years.  
delays. For a ﬁrst order cell delay model, estimation of correlations  
is feasible, as in [3]. However, ﬁrst order approximations do not  
describe very well the complex relationships between cell delays  
and process parameters, and this renders estimated correlations  
inaccurate. For a second order delay model, estimation of delay  
correlations from process parameters seems complex [4] due to  
their large number and the empirical nature of some of them in  
actual statistical model cards [8]. In addition, both weaknesses are  
compounded in the existing techniques of correlation estimation  
and render them crude. A recent solution, in the context of process  
parameters, involves bounding techniques [9].  
SSTA is richer than CTA in nature, while offering more accurate  
estimations of actual circuit performance. Rather than simply  
determine corners and attempt to obtain a single value for delays,  
statistical timing engines propagate Probability Density Function  
(PDF) of timing variables. A typical approach uses the ﬁrst [1–3]  
or second order [4–6] Taylor expansion of cell delays based on  
process variation models, like the grid [1] or the quad-tree  
model [2].  
According to the algorithm to explore timing graphs, the existing  
parametric SSTA fall into one of the two categories: block-based  
[1–6] and path-based SSTA [7,9–13]. Nowadays, the block-based  
algorithm gains favor due to the linearity of its runtime as a function  
of circuit size and the ability of handling false paths [13]. However,  
these advantages may be limited by accuracy losses due to the  
intensive use of the MAX/MIN operation and to the lack of an  
accurate timing model to handle multiple-input collisions [14].  
The path-based algorithm requires ﬁnding an appropriate set of  
candidate paths but avoids the MAX/MIN operation, while propagat-  
ing PDF along paths. Thus, it avoids sources of error during simple  
path timing estimate and offers a simple and rigorous framework to  
validate the main proposal of this work: an accurate and fast method  
to calculate delay correlations between cells along paths. This  
algorithm could eventually be applied in the block-based algorithm,  
provided techniques to perform accurately MAX/MIN operations and  
to propagate efﬁciently general PDF become available. Note that the  
proposed method remains valid for any set of paths. A complete  
path-based solution also requires solving the problem of extracting a  
However, digital circuit designers have been reluctant in  
adopting this new generation of timing analysis due to some  
remaining weaknesses.  
A ﬁrst weakness lies with the limited number of published delay  
models taking into account the effects of: Input/output (I/O) edges and  
output load values [7]. In fact, most approaches typically make a  
worst-case estimate of slope, which can lead to signiﬁcant errors [7].

Z. Wu et al. / Microelectronics Journal 43 (2012) 263–273  
proper set of paths. We do not focus on this important but alternate  
problem, and instead use the conventional method as explained in  
Section 3.  
Our method propagates iteratively means and variances of cell  
delay using conditional moments. These moments, conditioned on  
values of input slope and output load values, are pre-characterized by  
Monte Carlo (MC) simulations and organized as a tree of lookup  
tables, called a statistical timing library. This step is a one-time job as  
the high time-cost simulations are only needed once to build the  
library.  
3. Flow of the SSTA framework  
As shown in Fig. 1, the ﬂow of our framework is divided into  
four parts: INPUT, SETUP, SSTA ENGINE and OUTPUT. INPUT  
deﬁnes environmental conditions and for a given circuit design,  
extracts a set of candidate paths using RTL Compiler [16] based on  
a CTA considering the worst timing corner. SETUP is the proce-  
dure of constructing a statistical timing library with data from  
characterizations. SSTA ENGINE, fed by the library, estimates  
delay correlations and path delay distributions. OUTPUT gener-  
ates a report. More details about this ﬂow are given in the  
subsections below.  
The technique proposed here, which is based on the timing  
library, allows estimating cell-to-cell and path-to-path delay  
correlations via a classiﬁcation of process parameters. Correla-  
tions estimated by this technique depend on:  
a) supply voltage and temperature,  
b) cell topology and polarity,  
3.1. Setup  
c) input slope and output load values.  
The data needed to construct a statistical timing library comes  
from characterizations done with statistical process models under  
HSPICE [17]. In this paper, the 130 nm and the 65 nm process  
models are provided by industry, which is more realistic than  
relying on theoretical model. Built into the code of these models  
are the following two constraints:  
This technique is applied to the interpretation of the discre-  
pancies of critical paths orderings obtained by our SSTA frame-  
work and CTA.  
Another feature of our approach is the use of an improved  
method to characterize cell-timings. Instead of linear ramp and  
capacitor models, we use the log-logistic Cumulative Distribution  
Function (CDF) and active load (inverters) models for input slope  
and output load during the library statistical characterizations.  
These improvements provide increased accuracy, especially when  
capturing random variations of delays and slopes.  
a) any two process parameters are uncorrelated,  
b) there is no spatial correlation,  
which are imposed by industry (for some ideas on how to consider  
spatial correlations, see [18]). Fig. 2 shows that the statistical  
timing library has a tree structure with the following levels: cell  
type, I/O pin, I/O edge, temperature, supply voltage and timing  
variables (including cell delay gd and output slope tout).  
The tree leaves are lookup tables, referenced by given values of  
the pair of random variables tin and Cout, selected as follows. The  
range [T1,TI] of tin is partitioned by T1oT2o?oTio?oTI.  
Similarly, the range [C1,CJ] of Cout is partitioned by C1o  
C2o?oCjo?oCJ. These index values are selected by empirical  
design experience. The body of the tables contains the lookup  
The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews condi-  
tional moments. Section 3 introduces the ﬂow of the proposed  
framework, including the main techniques of the timing engine:  
moments propagation and estimation of delay correlations.  
Section 4 focuses on the improved method of timing character-  
izations. Section 5 discusses validation. Section 6 gives applica-  
tions. Finally, Section 7 concludes the paper.

2. Conditional moments

of tin and c of Cout, to approximate the conditional moments of tout  
Before proceeding, we brieﬂy review conditional moments,  
which are the core elements of our SSTA framework. Let X and Y  
be two random variables. The conditional mean E(X9Y¼y) is the  
mean of X given that Y has taken the value y. Unlike the  
conventional mean, which is constant for a given probability  
distribution, E(X9Y¼y) is a function of y. Similarly, the conditional  
variance Var(X9Y¼y) is the variance of X given that Y has taken the  
value y.  
and gd. Details are given in the following section.  
If Y follows a continuous distribution with PDF f(y), then the  
mean mX and the variance s2X of X can be related to E(X9Y¼y),  
Var(X9Y¼y) by the following formulas [15]:  
Z
mX ¼ EðXÞ ¼ E½EðX9YÞꢀ ¼ EðX9Y ¼ yÞfðyÞdy  
ð1Þ  
s2X ¼ VarðXÞ ¼ E½VarðX9YÞꢀþVar½EðX9YÞꢀ

ð2Þ  
Eqs. (1) and (2), which also hold when Y is a vector, give  
alternative ways to compute mX and s2X if the PDF of X is not  
explicitly known. These equations require a relationship between  
X and Y, and this dependency allows implementing moments  
propagation.

The three steps of the propagation procedure (lookup, inter-  
polate, compute), shown in Fig. 4, are repeated iteratively for all  
cells along a path starting from the ﬁrst. An important feature is  
that only the ﬁrst two moments (not the distributions) need to be  
known. For example, tin and tout may follow any two parameters  
continuous distribution on condition that its mean and variance  
exist, like Uniform, Gaussian, Log-Normal, etc.  
Similarly, mgd and s2gd can be computed by replacing E(tout9Ti,c),  
Var(tout9Ti,c), respectively, with E(gd9Ti,c) and Var(gd9Ti,c) in  
(7)–(10).  
Fig. 2. Structure of the statistical timing library.  
3.2. SSTA Engine  
3.2.2. Path delay distribution  
Because the moments propagation presented above allows  
computing cell delay moments associated to a path of L cells  
(l¼1, y, L), the path delay pd, which is the sum of all cell delays  
gdl, has mean mpd and variance s2pd given by expressions (11)–(12)  
from standard probability theory  
We now introduce the main techniques of the engine:  
moments propagation and estimation of delay correlations.

3.1). The effects of cell type, I/O edge and output load on CDCs can  
be important. As an example, the coefﬁcients pertaining to OR2  
decrease when output load values increase while for INV this  
trend is less pronounced and marred by the statistical noise of the  
simulations. For better accuracy, we need a technique that takes  
into account these effects.  
As cell delay depends on a number of factors, we propose a  
technique to estimate directly CDC. This technique avoids hand-  
ling complex relationships between delay and process parameters.  
The statistical model card used in the present work contains  
one model for N transistors and one model for P transistors with  
the usual (BSIM4 [8]) disjoint groups of process parameters, PN  
and PP, respectively, for each transistor type. To link the process  
variations of N and P transistors, an additional group of para-  
meters, denoted PS and disjoint from both PN and PP, is embedded  
in the statistical model card. It contains parameters characterizing  
the fabrication steps that are common to both P and N transistors.  
More precisely, the M process parameters p1, p2, y, pM are  
classiﬁed into the three groups

3.2.3. Cell-to-cell delay correlation  
A common way to estimate CDC is to approximate the  
dependency of cell delay on process parameters with a ﬁrst order  
Taylor expansion [3], and then to translate the correlation  
between process parameters into correlation between cell delays.  
However, besides process parameters, other factors affect cell  
delay, like cell type, output load, etc., and should be taken into  
account when estimating delay correlations. Table 1 shows that  
CDCs vary along with cell type (INV, BUF , OR2), output load values  
(1fF, 10fF, 100fF) and I/O edge (R/F, F/R, R/R,F/F). The CDCs in this  
table were estimated with data from MC simulations (1500 runs  
with the 65 nm statistical process models described in Section  
Then, each delay component in (14) is divided into uncorre-

4.1. Input signal model  
We focus on rising edges for simplicity, because rising and  
falling edges are similar in terms of shape.  
The notations used in the deﬁnition of LL-based models are  
given in Fig. 5. A signal is divided into two segments: t0rtrtmin  
and t4tmin. The idea is to describe the segment t0rtrtmin by a  
linear function, and the other by the LL CDF

two paths made of K1 and K2 cells. Then the PDC is

4. Timing characterization  
indicates the location of the approximated signal. Finally,

Timing characterization is the step that prepares data for the  
construction of the statistical timing library of our SSTA engine.  
During this step, we need models to describe input signal and  
output load.  
D
D
b
parameter tin, is retained. To see this we build the functions  
8
The standard method uses the linear ramp model and capaci-  
tors. Because this method is now known (see also Table 5) to yield  
important underestimate of means and variances of timing  
variables, it cannot be used to validate our SSTA engine.  
In order to circumvent this problem, we consider an input  
signal model based on the Log-Logistic (LL) Cumulative Distribution  
Function (CDF), and use inverters in place of capacitors for output  
load. This CDF has an easy to compute explicit expression as  
opposed to the CDF of the Log-Normal [19] or that of the Weibull  
[20], while offering comparable accuracy of ﬁt. Moreover, as will  
be seen in Section 5.3, the LL-based model with inverters  
produces slight overestimated standard deviations on delay,  
which allow us to validate our SSTA engine.

We now compare the accuracy of the linear ramp and LL-based  
models in the following way:  
4.2. Output load variations  
a) collect 1000 simulated signals under various conditions for cell  
types: INV, BUF, NOR2, NAND2;  
The main drawback in modeling output load with capacitors  
having constant values is that they cannot capture output load  
variations, or more precisely, the impact of load variations on  
cell timings; in real circuit, the charges of input pins of all cells  
depend on process parameters and therefore are random variables.  
Moreover, the mean and variance of output load may depend on  
interconnects and the subsequent cells [23] because of an I/O  
coupling phenomenon that generates overshoots (or undershoots)  
[21], [22]. Modeling this dependency is necessary to observe  
accurately the values c of Cout. This requires further work unrelated  
to the current SSTA proposal and is not pursuit further here.  
To circumvent this and better represent what occurs in the next  
stage of a given cell in real circuits, our timing characterization uses  
inverters instead of capacitors to model output load. The basic idea  
is to embed the impact of output load random variations, but also  
b) for each signal, measure the 20–80% slope and approximate  
the signal respectively by linear ramp and LL-based model;  
c) normalized all measured and approximated signals by their  
own slope s, i.e. rescale the t axis of Fig. 5 to t/s;  
d) transform the point (t0/s, 0.5Vdd) of each signal to point  
(0, 0.5Vdd), i.e. take tn¼(t–t0)/s, as shown in Fig. 6;  
e) deﬁne the grid of points t*¼ À1.5,À1.4, y, 0, y, 1.4,1.5, and  
compute errors point by point for both linear ramp and LL-  
based approximations;  
f) compute the average errors of the following three signal sub-  
samples: all linear ramp approximations, LL-based approxima-  
tions with slopes, respectively, less than 100 ps and 200 ps,  
and all LL-based approximations.

means and standard deviations of path delays are respectively less  
than 5% and 10%, thus validating the SSTA framework. Moreover,  
most of the standard deviations are slightly overestimated. This will  
reduce the probability of violating the setup time and hold time  
constraints if ICs are designed with this engine. Regarding CPU time,  
on any given path, our SSTA engine is on average 105 times faster  
than a corresponding 1500 runs Monte Carlo simulation.  
Fig. 8 illustrates this procedure. M inverters are connected at  
the output pin of this cell. The sum of input charges of all  
inverters corresponds to the output load indexes C1,C2, y, Cj in  
the lookup tables. Such a replacement gives better accuracy as  
will be shown later.  
5.2. Validation of the techniques to estimate correlations  
We now validate the techniques to estimate CDCs and PDCs.

We applied the path-based SSTA engine to the ITC99 benchmark  
circuits implemented in the 65 nm process. Note that, according to  
the setup described in Section 3.1, we consider only process  
variations, including 24 process parameters (channel length and  
width, oxide thickness, etc.), which are Gaussianly or Uniformly  
distributed as imposed by industry. But our engine is distribution  
free. The scale of variations is also imposed by industry.  
in panel (a) are located in the dashed region corresponding to eabs  
r0.2. In addition, most of the points outside this dashed region  
corresponds to overestimated r, which results in overestimating  
^
standard deviations of path delays (see Eq. (12)). This explains why,  
in Table 2, the majority of relative errors of path delay standard  
deviations are positive. In Fig. 9(b), PDCs are mostly in the region  
where erelr10%, which validates the reasoning in (22)–(25).  
In order to obtain more information about these accuracies, we  
^
To validate the SSTA engine, we ﬁrst did timing characteriza-  
tions and constructed the statistical timing library. Then, some  
critical paths were extracted from the considered circuits using  
CTA under RTL Compiler [16]. Next, we performed SSTA on these  
paths with the timing engine described in Section 3.2. Finally, we  
ran MC simulations (under HSPICE) on the same set of critical  
paths for comparisons.  
computed the percentages Poe, Pue of  
r
overestimating and under-  
estimating the corresponding r, respectively. Table 3 shows that  
81% of the CDCs are overestimated while 84% of the PDCs are  
underestimated. This is preferable. To see why, we turn to the  
problem of timing veriﬁcation. For ease of description, consider  
the setup time constraint  
The three considered circuits were b01, b05 and b07 of the ITC99  
benchmark. Table 2 shows that the relative errors on estimated  
pddataÀpdclk oTCLK  
ð33Þ  
270  
Z. Wu et al. / Microelectronics Journal 43 (2012) 263–273  
where s2data s2clk are, respectively, the variance of pddata and pdclk  
,
;
rdc is the PDC between pddata and pdclk and varies in the interval  
[0,1] in reality.  
According to (34), Var(pddataÀpdclk) will increase if one or  
more of the following cases appear:  
a) sdata increases,  
b) sclk increases,  
c) rdc decreases.  
In other words, both the overestimation of CDCs and the under-  
estimation of PDCs result in the overestimation of Var(pddataÀpdclk).  
Therefore, if the distribution with overestimated variance satisﬁes  
the setup time constraint, then so will the actual distribution. Thus,  
the overestimation on Var(pddataÀpdclk) is slightly pessimistic but  
preferable to any underestimation.  
5.3. Comparisons  
Table 4 shows the impacts of CDC and slope variations on the  
accuracy of estimated standard deviations. In addition to the  
technique to estimate CDC through (16) and (21), the following  
two extreme cases were considered:  
a) rkl¼1 for any two cells k and l,  
b) rkl¼0 except for k¼l.  
As shown in Table 4, these two cases respectively lead to an  
average relative error of 23.7% and À56.3%. The magnitude of this  
range shows the importance of estimating more precisely CDCs.  
The last column shows an average error of À2.7% when slope  
variations are not taken into account, leading to a difference of  
about 8% compared with the proposed approach.  
Finally, Table 5 demonstrates the beneﬁts of using the models  
in Section 4. It compares standard deviations of path delays  
estimated by data from MC simulations, which are considered  
as reference values, with those computed using statistical timing  
libraries based on the following combinations of input signal and  
output load models:  
a) LL-based signal and inverters,  
b) linear signal and inverters,  
c) LL-based signal and capacitors.  
In Table 5, attention must be focused on the following  
comparisons: combinations (a) vs (b) and combinations (a) vs  
(c). The average relative errors show a difference in percentage of  
about 16 between combinations (a) and (b), and about 12  
between combinations (a) and (c). Note that the column corre-  
sponding to combination (b) (and also combination (c)) shows  
that they are all underestimated, which is unexpected in case of  
SSTA. From these results, we may conclude that both LL-based  
input signal and inverters output load improve the accuracy of  
computing path delay standard deviations.

Table 6  
Average delay gains of the SSTA engine over CTA (without interconnects).  
6.2. Ordering of critical paths  
At the present time, most IC designers still use tools based on  
path-based CTA, and consider SSTA as a complement to CTA.  
However, results of SSTA do not necessarily coincide with those of  
CTA even when considering a single voltage Vdd and temperature  
T corner [25].  
A well known reason explaining such differences, is the effect  
of path re-convergence that induces topological correlations  
between paths and creates false paths [13]. Block-based SSTA  
offers a solution to take this effect into account, but does not offer  
a solution to manage simultaneously process, temperature and  
voltage variations.

12.3  
average gains of delays:  
This section shows there are at least two additional sources of  
discrepancy between orderings obtained with SSTA and CTA. They  
appear even on an isolated path. One of these sources could  
explain the rare unexpected optimism of CTA pointed out in [25].  
A typical Computer-Aided-Design (CAD) tool based on CTA, like  
RTL Compiler [16], extracts a set of N critical paths for optimiza-  
tion of circuit design. These N critical paths are ordered by  
decreasing worst delay according to CTA, i.e. the ﬁrst critical path  
has the maximal worst delay; the second one has the second  
maximal value, etc. However, if path delays of the same set are  
computed by SSTA, the ordering of these paths may be different  
with that of CTA.

agrees with technology roadmaps [24] that predict these  
average gains of delays will become more and more important  
as the feature size shrinks from 65 nm to 45 nm, 32 nm, etc.

of rkm. In the same manner, going from (42) to (37) eliminates the

and from (42) to (38a) eliminates the inﬂuence of rkm

Fig. 10 plots the four curves wpd, spd, s0pd, s*pd. The rank of each  
path corresponds to decreasing values of wpd. The two curves wpd  
and s*pd have a similar shape, and the same holds for the pair of  
curves spd and s0pd. These similarities allow concluding that, in this  
case, the discrepancy of orderings comes mainly from yk, i.e. from  
the pessimistic standard deviation values considered by CTA.  
Indeed, 81% of the average gap between wpd (CTA) and spd (SSTA)  
comes from the difference between wpd and s0pd. Note that the  
above paths have logical depths ranging from 10 to 16. This  
conclusion is further supported by Fig. 11 that shows the evolu-  
tion of ðwpdÀsnpdÞ=ðwpdÀspdÞ% with respect to logical depth, i.e. the  
percentage of CTA pessimism explained by CDCs. For most of the  
paths, this percentage is around 30%, except for some short paths.  
From this ﬁgure, one may conclude that accurate estimates of  
CDCs are mandatory for accurate estimates of path delays,  
especially when logical depths are less than 10.

Comparing (37) with (38), the quantities rkm are ﬁxed to the  
constant ‘‘1’’, meaning that all cell delays are perfectly correlated.  
In (38a), sgd Uyk=3 are the pessimistic standard deviation values  
considered by CTA, and thus are the duals of sgd in (37).  
Therefore, the discrepancy of orderings comes from two factors:  
cell-to-cell delay correlation rkm and standard deviation of cell  
delay sgd  
k
k
.
k
On the one hand, in the context of timing analysis, rkm varies  
in the interval [0,1], and it is rare that rkm¼1 if kam. However,  
rkm is systematically set to ‘‘1’’ in (38), which introduces a ﬁrst  
7. Conclusions  
difference between wpd and spd  
.
In this paper, we have developed a path-based SSTA framework  
that allows a fast estimation of path delay distributions and delay  
correlations by propagating iteratively mean and variance of cell  
delay. Its accuracy derives mainly from a realistic estimation of  
delay correlations and the use of log-logistic and active load  
(inverters) models for input slope and output load. As a result, the  
proposed engine gives estimates of path delay means and standard  
deviations with relative errors less than 5% and 10%, respectively.  
Compared to CTA, our SSTA framework yields a delay gain of about  
12% in 65 nm technology. Finally, the application of our SSTA  
framework shows that main sources of pessimism of CTA are  
On the other hand, according to the CTA, wgd is computed by  
k
setting each process parameter pl to a corner value. Therefore it is  
unlikely that yk is exactly equal to 3 in (38a), which introduces a  
second source of difference in orderings.  
To identify which of these two factors has more inﬂuence, we  
compute s0pd and s*pd with

b) the assumption that cell-to-cell delay correlations are unrea-  
listically set to ‘‘1’’.
